items.question: What goes in `[build-system]`?
items.answer: Two keys: `requires` (build dependencies installed in an isolated env) and `build-backend` (the module pip calls to produce a wheel). E.g. `requires = ['setuptools>=64','wheel']` and `build-backend = 'setuptools.build_meta'`.

items.question: What goes in `[project]`?
items.answer: Project metadata standardised by PEP 621: `name`, `version` (or `dynamic = ['version']`), `description`, `readme`, `requires-python`, `license`, `authors`, `classifiers`, `dependencies`, `optional-dependencies`, `urls`, `scripts`. Backend-agnostic.

items.question: How are tool configs organised?
items.answer: Each tool reads its own `[tool.<name>]` table. Common examples: `[tool.pytest.ini_options]`, `[tool.mypy]`, `[tool.ruff]`, `[tool.coverage.run]`, plus backend-specific `[tool.setuptools.*]` or `[tool.hatch.*]`.

items.question: Why prefer `pyproject.toml` over `setup.py`?
items.answer: Declarative not executable (safer to read/static-analyse), standardised across backends, supports isolated builds, consolidates tool config in one place, no `setup.py` magic to debug.

items.question: How does `pip install .` use it?
items.answer: Pip reads `[build-system]`, installs `requires` into an isolated env, calls `build-backend.build_wheel(...)`, then installs the resulting wheel into the target environment. Source code is never executed by pip itself.

items.question: What's an entry point and how is it declared?
items.answer: An entry point is a named hook to a Python callable. `[project.scripts] cli = 'pkg.cli:main'` exposes `cli` as a command on PATH. `[project.entry-points.'group'] name = 'pkg.module:obj'` registers plugins for any consumer that looks up that group.
